videos.summary: What’s new in Gemma 4? focuses on Open Source Models, AI Agents, Model Releases. Key transcript signal: Built from the same world-class research and technology behind Gemini 3, Gemma 4 is a family of open models designed to run directly on the hardware you own: phone, laptops, [music] and desktop.
videos.title: What’s new in Gemma 4?
videos.transcript_text: We are thrilled to announce Gemma [music] 4. Built from the same world-class research and technology behind Gemini 3, Gemma 4 is a family of open models designed to run directly on the hardware you own: phone, laptops, [music] and desktop. For the first time ever, we're releasing Gemma under an open-source Apache 2.0 license. Gemma 4 is built for the agentic era. It can handle complex logic, multi-step [music] planning, and agentic workflows, making optimal use of tokens for its intelligence. The bigger model perform well with a context [music] window of up to 1/4 million tokens, allowing you to analyze entire code bases, and all multi-turn agentic use cases. It features native support for tool use, allowing you to build agents that plan and act on [music] your behalf. Let's break down the model family now. First, a 26B mixture of experts [music] and a 31B dense models. These provide frontier intelligence directly on your personal computer. You can run state-of-the-art local reasoning and coding pipeline without needing to upload data outside of your control environment. The 26B MoE with 3.8 billion activated parameters is exceptionally fast, while 31B is optimized for output quality. Then, we have our effective 2B and effective 4B models. Engineered for maximum memory efficiency, these models bring a whole new level of intelligence to mobile and IoT devices. [music] With combined audio and vision support for real-time processing, they can see and hear the world. All of this while natively supporting over 140 languages. Now, [music] let's test our effective 2B on multilingual and agentic tasks. Hey, Gemma. Est-ce que tu peux trouver un restaurant français à San ? Please reply to me in English. Amazing, we have a winner. As open models become more central to enterprise infrastructure, security is paramount. Developed by Google DeepMind, Gemma 4 undergoes the same rigorous security protocols >> [music] >> as our proprietary models, giving enterprise and developer a trusted foundation to build on top of. We want you to be able to use Gemma 4 with the tools you already know and love. You can download the weights and start experimenting today. [music] We cannot wait to see what you create next.

videos.summary: How Ramp built an AI agent that can think outside of tokens | Alex Shevchenko focuses on AI Agents, Coding Assistants, Open Source Models. Key transcript signal: We go deep into the self monitoring loop behind sheets and how ramp's internal coding agent inspect fits into it.
videos.title: How Ramp built an AI agent that can think outside of tokens | Alex Shevchenko
videos.transcript_text: Maybe we can just like finish the loop so that they just like record the video and create the automation themselves. >> Today I'm talking to Alexander Shoshenko, head of applied AI research at RAMP sheets. Their AI spreadsheet editor has been getting a lot of traction and we dive into the architecture decisions underneath it. >> One of the approaches that's like easiest and probably like more accurate is still doing codegen and then just like plopping it back in the Excel like view. We decided not to do that. We go deep into the self monitoring loop behind sheets and how ramp's internal coding agent inspect fits into it. >> The agent deems them to be like good enough and they get like kind of promoted to start bugging the engineers. >> Alex then goes into some of the experiments they did around recursive language models >> by having instead of them communicates in token space the actual orchestrator in the ROM is closed source. So it was aanthropic pod family orchestrator but then the worker agents were open sourced. We also get into some of the experiments that ramp is running around steering vectors. >> It would almost become self-aware around like its interactions or like why am I talking about spaghetti when the question is about like the meaning of life. >> Welcome to Max Agency, the podcast that goes deep into how the best agents are being built by builders like you. I'm really excited to chat with you today because I think you guys are famous at this point for doing a lot of really cool stuff in the AI engineering space. One of the things that I want to dive into is sheets agent or that's what I call it. I don't know if it has a more official name. >> RAM sheets. >> RAM sheets. Yeah. So I think you guys launched that in November. Um what was the what was the origin story of that and and why build ramp sheets? >> Yeah. So we actually arrived at it through a very long like evolutionary process of trying to address the needs of our own internal finance team. And so initially it started out as this process mining project where we were just trying to understand what were the things that our own finance team was doing, what were our accountants were doing as part of the like mundend close tasks and we would do like this video to like markov diagram process to try to just map it out so that engineers could then pick it up as a piece of documentation and try to automate it on the finance person's behalf. And that turned out to to work super well. >> Can you talk a little bit more about that process? Like you would video to Marov diagram. >> Yeah. >> What what what's the video of and then how did that process work? >> Actually, one of the like richest ways of communicating in any company still to this day, but especially like a year ago, um is like recording a Loom video of you going through something and just sharing it because it's like the like kind of easiest way of of creating an artifact. it takes a lot less work um to just show around and click around a spreadsheet and show how you do something compared to like trying to write out a nice clean notion doc of the process or trying to like formalize it as an SOP. And so a lot of comm like communication between like accountants but also like engineers and kind of like everyone is like you record a loom, you send it off and then the person watches it. >> And would that include like a voice over or the person talking as well? >> Yeah, exactly. video of them walking through like an Excel and and audio of them narrating it. It is very easy to produce, but it's kind of harder to consume than a notion doc. And it's like the more effort you put into producing that artifact, the easier it becomes to digest for the person, but the more work it is on the person producing it. And our finance team is like very very busy. And so they don't have necessarily that much time to like produce these artifacts for our software engineering team for us to automate it. And so we tried to to basically build something out so that to facilitate our work of consuming those looms that are very very like highly technical but in the finance like world. Um, and so getting that financial context into our our own heads by getting this pipeline working that would take that loom video of them speaking and and explaining a process and then generating basically a text document and this like process diagram of just like node starting like I open this uh bank account, I pulled the invoice from it, I open this other system of record, maybe I open like the GL or something and I try to reconcile between the three and they have a bunch of these like one-off tasks that they run every month. And so we started building out kind of like this library of tasks that are like well documented off of these Loom videos. And the idea then was well we'll take a software engineer and sit them down and they would just like automate them one at a time by writing like a Python script or something. At that point, we thought, well, maybe we can just like finish the loop and take what we have built and do like codegen or something to automate it away for the financial professionals so that they just like record the video and create the automation themselves. And we tried a few different approaches of it like just Python codegen, we tried NAN, um we tried like retool automations and kind of the feedback that we got was all of these are kind of too blackbox for the finance professional. Like obviously this is like very high stakes kind of like a asymmetrical thing where like saving time is great but making a mistake is to like disproportionately bad. And so this blackbox approach really like was not good enough for them. >> It would be blackbox because they would pass in an Excel spreadsheet and get back something and have >> blackbox in the from the like finance person's perspective. Like it's not a black box for us because it's like Python code um that we could probably verify, but if we're trying to automate it as much as possible, we want to get it end to end automated and Python is like a great medium that's very expressive. But to them it's a black box because >> they're not going to understand. >> Yeah, they're not going to understand like the pandas import and the data frames and how they're they're being like uh transformed. And so we took a step back from that and like the the feedback like how can we work around that feedback? And we started just watching the looms ourselves um of them doing the work. And it's interesting because you drop in your cursor at kind of like any point into one of their videos and like 99% of the time they're in a spreadsheet. Like there's some amount of time at the beginning while they where they like load it up from like a bank account or like the GL or whatever, but then the majority of the time it's just them going through an Excel spreadsheet and like making modifications or creating like a worksheet off of it to make the reconciliations or something. And so we decided, well, we should probably meet them where they already are, which is the spreadsheet. And so we took that process mining like video like process generation pipeline and built out like the V2 version of the automation, but instead of doing codegen, we decided to replicate the exact same way as as they perform the work, which is opening an Excel spreadsheet and making the modifications directly in it. and taking those learnings of like not making it a black box. Like one of the approaches that's like easiest and and probably like more accurate is still doing codegen and then just like popping it back into the Excel like view. We decided not to do that. we decided to make it like agentic spreadsheet modifications in the sense of like read this column, read this row, write this row um and with Excel formulas in the spreadsheet to really like try to capture the exact same workflow as they do. And that was much much better received because well they right away get a lot more visibility into what's happening because they are professionals at parsing Excel spreadsheets. like they're better at reading all those formulas and how they they fit together than like we are at reading code. Um, and so they they became a lot more receptive of that uh medium and that was used somewhat internally and and we looked at it and we realized like maybe we ship this out into the world. And as we prepared like with Ramp Labs to like package this up in a nice way and and send it out into the world, we we thought to ourselves, well, RAMP has like these amazing processes for like month clothes and like all our like ducks are in a row basically from the finance side. That's not necessarily true for a lot of companies, especially like early stage startups. They don't necessarily have a process to automate yet. they are in the like they need to create that workbook from scratch. And so we started looking at like maybe we just take that automation piece and we package that up without the process mining piece because well there is probably not that much to process mine for a lot of companies as is and it's like more complex setup and just like the first time user experience is like nicer if you can just pop in and just be like build me a DCF or something. Um, and so that's what was launched in November, which we called RAM Sheets, which is like this agentic spreadsheet editor. >> I want to talk more about RAM sheets, but first I want to talk a little bit more about the process mining stuff. Like how far did that get you for ramp sheets? Like did you have this process down where you would get these looms, drop them into the system, some system you created, and out would come an agent that could do all of these things. Like what did that process look like? And if and if it was an agent at the end, like how good was that agent? how much like more tuning did you have to do on top of it? >> Yeah, I mean it depends on on the task at hand. Um, so for like very complex like if it's like FPNA and if it's something that's like out of the distribution of things that the model was RLED on, it's not going to perform super well. Even if you if even if you give it the really good context like LM kind of like tend to go towards like middle of distribution outputs like we we didn't get super far in in those like strategic finance workflows but accounting and like reconciliations and bookkeeping those are types of things that are very repeatable. Uh the context like you can probably get most of the context in and and you don't need to like any cleverness or originality about it. It's just like a very strict process. So those types of things are like if you map out the process and you get the the right pieces of context to the agent, it's able to replicate it pretty well. >> And so what was the output of this process mining thing? >> Yeah. So it was a couple of things. It was like we kind of from the same video outputed in multiple modalities. One was just like a text um description of the process itself. That's like as simple as like it's like the equivalent of like today if you go into like I don't know like Gemini uh like studio and you drop in a video and you ask it to like summarize it. That's kind of like the textual representation of like as far as it went. Then there was like a more complex thing where we would create like this kind of like directed graph of nodes of like well first you open like your bank account to pull invoices and then that invoice will get used for like the sheet like sheet one in this workbook and then you need to get your ledger and this is going to be like sheet two and so kind of like this directed graph of dependencies and like work to be done >> and did you have some DSL for this for this DAG or how is that represented This is like almost naive but it worked out well. We we basically generated like graph wiz language for it. So like the the library for for making like dot graphs um and we would just have it generate that but with a lot of constraints around like the graph that we wanted it to output. Um and that's what that's what would like generate that process mind diagram. Do you think this is how all agents will be created in the future? You make some Loom video, you drop it in, it creates a scale or or a graph whiz thing or something like that and boom, there you go. >> Yeah. Nowadays, I would replace like a few of those pieces with something like kind of like more modern, more 2026 of uh building out skills or or building out something that is like more quad code native. I think the process mining piece was is still interesting. Um, we actually haven't been like doing as much like experimentation to develop it further, but it's still good for like you take a video and you get you produce like an artifact of documentation that you can have some like store somewhere and then have it consumed by like cloud code or like cloud co-work maybe not end to end uh automations but just like as you are working and as you're like pulling in context um that ends up being like a pretty good workflow as well. >> Maybe talking about the ramp sheets agent a little bit more. So, so what did it look like under the hood? You already mentioned some of the tools it had, but like what was the architecture? Was it was it similar in architecture to like cloud code, this LLM running in a loop with tools? Did it have access to a file system? How was how is the spreadsheet represented? We have a like an agent SDK that runs the loop. Um then we spin up kind of like a modal sandbox in which we have um spreadjs which is like this uh Excel manipulation uh library that loads it up and then is able to like perform modifications on it and the agent will have like specific tools like read range, set range, format range and >> and those are given like as actual tools not as like functions in a ripple or or scripts in a coding environment or >> Yeah. directly as tools. Um, but they they interact with like the spreadsheet inside of the sandbox. The the reason for the sandbox is that there is like still at the end of the day like an escape hatch for doing some codegen stuff. So sometimes people want to do things that aren't necessarily like Excel shaped or Excel native, but we still want them to be able to do it in RAM sheets. to like do doing like a large cleanup or something that lend still lends itself better and when it's not like a month and close task or something that like someone has like a list of vendors and they want to just like filter out filter it out easily on a bunch of clauses. It's like still better on with Python and they're like less reluctant around it being like a blackbox thing. So it's still like running in a sandbox. We can do codegen. We can execute it against it. But most of like the actual like finance or accounting uh shaped stuff is done pure like function call of like read range. Um and then the like agent will try to systematically as a human would like kind of like read in diagonals with like these kind of like blocks of it's not going to read the entire thing so that we don't pollute the entire context. It reads in one spot, then it reads further down just as a as a human would and then it would produce like a another sheet for example by writing to it with references to cells from the sheets that it discovered or like columns. Do you know do you have any clue how often it used the Python tool versus the the normal Excel tools that you gave it? We try to like forcibly like make it use it the least possible like just as a last resort escape hatch for things that are not possible just through pure Excel formulas like we really try to bias it towards Excel formulas. There's stuff that's like outside of the like things that are possible within Excel. So as a percentage it would be like 95 5% maybe for the Excel tools that you gave it. Was this like order of magnitude like five tools, 10 tools, 20 tools? How many different kind of like yeah specific tools for specific capabilities did you give it in Excel? >> Uh Excel wise I think it was around like 10ish tools maybe. So not super super large tool set >> but different than maybe like bash which is one universal tool or something like We found that like the especially the enthropic family of models is like really good at agentic spreadsheet manipulations especially back when we launched it. The latest models from like OpenAI have been like catching up around it but you could like kind of clearly tell that the entropic models were like really rled on on being able to like decompose Excel tasks into like specific actions to take on on the spreadsheet one by one. I think there's some idea in coding that you want to like line up like if you're building your own harness, you want to line up the tools that you give it similar to what the models have been RLED on. And so that's kind of like similar tools to whatever's in cloud code or something like that. Do you know if the 10 or so Excel tools that you gave it were like did you talk with the Entropic team and were like, "Hey, what did you RL it on and can we write the same type of tools or was it like close enough probably and so it probably just worked?" >> We launched it in November. that was like before we got access to like cloud for Excel or anything. So we kind of had to like rediscover it from first principles ourselves and the initial version was kind of like more open AI leaning and then we discovered like the cloud family just tends to perform much better on the way that we had developed the tools and think it ended up replicating it quite well their internal environments. >> Did you confirm that with them or is that just a guess because it worked so well? >> Yeah, it's a guess because it works so well but it does work very well. >> You mentioned an agent SDK. what agent SDK did you use? >> So, it was like OpenAI agent SDK that we started out on. Um, but it was kind of like this ship of TCS thing almost as like we went further and further where we had to like rip out pieces one piece at a time and just like customize it. Like it was very very overfit to the task that we ended up like landing on. So it's kind of like this this Frankenstein harness of like >> we started out thinking that it was going to be like relatively simple but then as as the progress like as the project grew had to like replace a bunch of stuff. >> Question about the UX. Uh how do people interact with ramp sheets? Is it chat based? >> Yeah. So you have a spreadsheet editor on the left left hand. Um you can basically do anything you would in Excel write formulas etc in there. The one thing that's like agentic in there is like you can select for example a range of cells and like add to context that specific range and then there's a chat interface on the right that general like experience is just like you prompt it and you ask questions for for things to be done. There's like some templates that are customized for specific tasks um that someone will do like reconciliation. there's um some some like nicities in the chat where we'll like ask you follow-up questions or like try to plan where it's going to be like slightly generative interface where it's like a form to fill out to answer the questions. Um but for for the most part it's just like a chat interface. Yeah. >> How long does the agent take to run? >> That depends on on the task at hand. We have like a fast and expert mode. Um >> and the user toggles that. >> Yeah. the the user decides and yeah it depends on the complexity of the task. If you give it like some very complex task in FPNA of like get data from the web around like SEC filings or something aggregate it together and produce a model for me it can run for like 30 40 50 minutes for simpler things if you have like the process already defined if it doesn't need to like go and get out and get like information from anywhere can be very quick like can be 20 30 seconds. Do you have any sense of what the distribution is? Like what types of questions are people asking? Are they asking the complicated ones or the simple ones or somewhere in the middle? >> It's it's a mix of everything. I think the average session is probably around like 10ish minutes of >> And is and is a session like does that include multiple back and forths? Like I could kick off the agent multiple times within a session or is a session like one run of the agent from start to finish? >> One conversation tends to be around like 10 minutes. Um, but it's dominated kind of by like the the question answering piece of it where like out of the 10 minutes on average I think it would be like 7 minutes of like just the agent grinding on producing an output for you. There's this architecture kind of like question or debate of like agent in a sandbox or agent like with a sandbox as a tool. It sounds like actually yeah which one did you guys go for and and why? >> Uh, ours is the agent is outside of the sandbox. I don't know architecturally I I don't know if like our answer is even the correct one. I think that's why everyone's still debating around it. I think for us it was like often we will like just spin up the sandbox again so to to constrain like if there is a codegen step we don't want it to be polluted any in any way from the previous one and having the agent outside of the sandbox thing. I mean, you can it's kind of like this gradient of like you can probably do it in any way you want, but the one that we landed on is like the agent just keeps track of the state outside of the sandbox. And then if we need to like the codegen does not get polluted by previous runs because it gets spun up and the sandbox like applies the the coding steps uh the codegen code onto the like worksheet and then just goes back and doesn't get polluted every time. And you've got a sandbox per thread basic per session. >> So like per per kind of like agentic action. >> Oh, okay. So even more gray or >> I don't know if that's like the correct term like you you you have a conversation which I guess I would call a session. A user tells it to do something and that will have its sandbox based on like the input received from the user and then it will grind out on producing some output. Then that output get it goes out of the sandbox and then if the user tells it to do something again that will like get another sandbox >> a different sandbox. >> Yeah. >> Okay. But for multiple tool calls within the same grinding it out those all use the same sandbox. >> Yeah. >> How do you think about testing or just like gaining confidence that this thing actually like works? >> Yeah. I mean we have some like some labelled data sets from like experts. We like a lot of it you could call kind of call it like vibe evaling to some degree but it's like very educated vi vibe evaling because we had like input documents from the pro like someone doing a month closed task for example from a previous month and we know that what it looks like. So it was like kind of like the the VIB val was in the sense of like a human would or like an engineer would go in drop in that file, see what it outputs and compare it to like the v spreadsheet um without like using an LM as a judge or anything along those lines. >> Could you compare them exactly and like these need to be exactly the same and if they're at all wrong then this is wrong or is there some gray area? that that's where the complexity of it is and that's why like it was mostly done by an engineer that kind of like tries to understand like this is the way that we do like an AR reconciliation. You are able to evaluate it with an LLM as a judge but the amount of effort to produce like the serious eval of like this is the kind of like the data set, this is the task and this is the rubric becomes like pretty extensive and pretty expensive to produce. And so you can just kind of like use the human's judgment instead. Uh like if they have like a good understanding. The end of the day like an AR reconciliation is just like you go through line item by line item and you try to find a discrepancy and then try to explain the discrepancy. But like evaluating that like automated with an LLM as a judge with like a rubric that becomes like much more work to to build out. One of the very cool related things to sheets was this self-improvement, self-monitoring kind of loop that you guys wrote about. Would love to hear more about that. >> Yeah. So, this actually leverages like this internal coding agent that uh another team at REM has been developing called Inspect. And so, Inspect is like this large coding agent that is plugged into all of our systems. All of kind of like the MCPs preconfigured for you. uh snapshots kind of like all the environments like Rem Sheets, you can spin it up in in like a couple of seconds and and have the back end and front end running inside of it. And because it's like so well customized to how well we do coding at RAMP, uh there's some nicities around making automations on inspect. And so one of the like experiments that was very uh successful was one of our engineers Alex Levenson has created this like self-monitoring loop where he set up inspect to run an action whenever there's a new PR that gets created uh also like as a cron job at night to just instrument uh ramp sheets with extra data dog monitors so that we get like more signal of like things that break. Um and it's like a a pretty uh pretty large workflow and um re like viewers at home can go and read the blog post around all the intricacies of it. But yeah, basically either when a PR gets created or on the chron job, um it will go and it will like look at the code base and try to see if like there's a monitor that is missing or there's like a metric that we should be adding and then it goes into kind of like this this shadow mode where the monitor alerts won't like bug anyone yet, but then there's like another agent that will review them and if there's they're like too noisy then they get pruned because there's not enough signal. But if they run for a while and they're not noisy and the agent deems them to be like good enough, then they get like kind of promoted to start bugging the engineers and also like kind of like slacks us uh the results. >> How do you know if the signals it gets initially are like noisy or not? Like you could set up new monitors and there could be issues and that might not be noise. Like how do you determine whether that's >> Yeah, I mean the the handmade monitors that an engineer would would go and create those are obviously like they have like the human judgment in them. So those will just like alert right away. But the ones that are automated, they like in order for us not to get too much noise because like the V0 of this was extremely annoying and would just like spam our our channel that where we like do a lot of work. we're like, "No, this is too noisy. Like, create a new channel and like set up some filter." And the the blog post does go into detail around how like that filter was created to try to well minimize the amount of noise. And there's just like a couple of like huristics around like the types of things of like if the lat like it's like a latency thing and like you get alerted for the latency, but it's like something that you really don't care about or it's like something that is like the P90 or the P95 is like too large. But in the grand scheme of things, it's like expected SLA for end to end interactions. You're like, well, that's probably noise. And Opus is actually like pretty good at just like deciding what is noise and what isn't. >> And what is the end result of this? Is it kind of like a ping in Slack or does it does it open up a PR as well? Does it try to like fix the issue? >> It's both. So, it will ping us on Slack. um there's like this channel um where there's a bunch of these alerts and then yeah, it will do like a first pass at trying to create a GitHub uh PR to to try to fix it and they do get merged in quite a bit especially when they're like on the simpler end. Obviously, it's not going to do like oh your architecture has like this misstep and it's going to like create something large like those tend to not be mergeable but it's very good for alerting and for fixing like very small bugs that people run into. Have you seen this applied to any other teams or projects at ramp or is this unique for Sheets? >> So Sheets is like our kind of like playground for testing out these wacky ideas. But now Alex Levenson has been been spearheading this effort. He's uh productionizing and platformizing this so that it can be reused uh by other projects and he's like running another pilot pilot on um an internal product that is like 0ero to one uh that another team is building right now. What other wacky ideas have you run on sheets or experimented on sheets whether they've seen the light of day or or not? >> Yeah, I mean just like from experimenting I feel like like sheets is like this ship of thesis thing that we keep like rebuilding a lot of like context management of like how much data should you be giving it like we looked at like foviating what it sees. So like instead of like it having like a small 5x5 uh like cells that we show it, it's like it decides on like the range that it should be reading as an argument. Kind of like very specific things like that. I I don't even know there's like just so many small experiment that go into this and a lot of them get discarded over time. There's like another one in in kind of like memory management where we were like doing embedding based memory to try to help it out. There's memories that just like a naive tool call for memory creation kind of like Chad GPT does of like remember I want to always have like the first row be blank or the left like left column be blank for comments and it's like a tool called that would just go create a memory that gets passed in. Um that was like very similar to how chat GPT does it hasn't been like super super useful for some reason. Um so like looking through like user interactions hasn't been like bringing any like super helpful uh interactions >> and under the hood for that memory stuff was is it just a list of strings that is memory or is is it some other data structure >> for that specific like function call it was yeah it's like a string so it has like a function call basically of like if a user like explicitly says like this is how I do uh like I like to keep my left column uh blank for comments it will just go and like decide to make that tool call. That tool call just populates a memory bank and that memory bank gets injected with context for every future agent run. >> How does that memory bank get updated? Like if I say, you know, keep the first row blank and then the next day I'm like actually I want it to be two rows blank. What happens then? >> Well, that that one is like relatively simple. So like if it does get triggered in that specific interaction, it should like realize that it should go and update and that function call will go and just modify the kind of like scratch pad that it has for it. But to your point, it does run into like kind of like a lot of issues, especially for more complex flows of like because it's like such a rudimentary memory system. If you want it to be like, oh, when I am preparing my DCF, this is like the specific ways that I want to be linking cells and then they come back and they like add more like to that, it's because of how rudimentary it is, it's not necessarily going to know that it should go and like modify um that specific DCF memory in an appropriate way. I'm sure that there's like ways to improve it, but also like from user interactions, it doesn't seem to be like just like a flow that makes that much sense where people like want to have it remember that much stuff. >> You guys have been doing a bunch of other experiments as well at at Ramp Labs. Uh what are some of the recent ones that you're most excited about? >> Yeah, there's two uh that we published like last week and the week before that were really exciting. Uh the one from last week was called Leon Briefing. videos.summary: we JUST figured out how AI thinks focuses on AI Agents, Model Releases, Safety and Alignment. Key transcript signal: First and foremost, Meter Research finally publishes the Claude Mythos preview where it falls on their chart of how well AI agents are able to do certain tasks.
videos.title: we JUST figured out how AI thinks
videos.transcript_text: All right, there's a few big updates out of Enthropy today. First and foremost, Meter Research finally publishes the Claude Mythos preview where it falls on their chart of how well AI agents are able to do certain tasks. And I got to say that chart keeps getting scarier and scarier. Here is Claude Mythos. Anthropic recently also posted this article, Natural Language Autoenccoders: Turning Claude's Thoughts into Text. They learned to read Claude's mind. Here it is. What if we could read an AI model's thoughts? They figured out how to translate what happens in its neural activations into English text. And at the same time, they're launching this, the Anthropic Institute, TAI or TAI, a research organization trying to predict what the impacts of AI will be on the world. economic diffusion, threats and resilience, AI systems in the wild, and AIdriven research and development. That last point is very interesting right now and very relevant. Here's why. This is Jack Clark. He's the co-founder of Anthropic AI. Here's a little post that he did very recently saying, "I spent the past few weeks reading hundreds of public data sources about AI development. I now believe that recursive self-improvement has a 60% chance of happening by the end of 2028. In other words, AI systems might soon be capable of building themselves. One interesting thing that Eler Yutokovski is jumping in here responding to Jack Clark saying, "Then you'll die with the rest of us." Right? So if this artificial super intelligence, recursive self-improvement, if it happens, that's not going to be good for all of us. Eliaser is of course the author of the book if anyone builds it everyone dies and he's one of the main people kind of talking about the potential risks of AI. He's saying there will turn out to be some clever little gotchas in controlling ASI just like there were in building RBMK nuclear reactors and you will die. Go and tell the United Nations that if they do not shut down all AI companies they will die. Now, if you've been watching this channel for some time, of course, none of this is going to be surprising. None of this is going to be new. The rate of progress is exponential. These models are getting better. Their abilities are getting better. And we were expecting some sort of automated AI research by, you know, different numbers were thrown around maybe 2027, 2028. A lot of people in the space are expecting that we're approaching that time. And I think most people will admit that our AI progress is proceeding faster than our AI safety understanding. The our ability to do AI alignment that progress is a little bit slower than our progress in just increasing capabilities. And this is why Anthropic's research into alignment and understanding how to interpret the thoughts and actions of these AI models, why it was so interesting. I think this might be one of the most, if not the most, important interpretability papers that were published this year. So, the company says that they've built a natural language autoenccoder, NLA, and this can translate a model's internal activations into human readable text. In plain English, they figured out how to get what Claude is thinking in human readable text. Now, this isn't magic. This is early research. It's imperfect, but it it seems like a very positive sign. The direction is is huge because if this is true, then this would give Frontier AI Labs an ability to see what models are actually thinking, what they're planning, what their hidden motives might be. Okay, so let's break it down a little bit. So, first and foremost, what are activations? So Claude talks in words, but Claude doesn't think in words. This might be a little confusing because we have things like the chain of thoughts, right? So we might think of that kind of chain of thoughts as its internal thoughts. Not quite. It's more like a private diary where it writes out its plans and kind of like what it's thinking before it actually executes it. But it's not the same as its sort of internal activations. It's its inner thoughts inside the model. Everything is these long streams of numbers called activations. This is similar to neural activity in the brain. So if you see those MRI scans, you see like the electrical activity. So think of that as like human neuron activations cla and other large language models. Other AI models have something similar and they're just numbers. And one of the things that has scared a lot of people I think for a long time is that for most of this time that we've had these neural networks most of those numbers most of those neural nets it was just kind of a black box. We put tons of data in one side and we asked it to predict certain things you know from the other side whether it's to predict the next word in a sentence or you know predict what a picture of a cat would look like when you type in picture of a cat. That's what inference is. it's it's predictions. We're teaching it to predict what something might look like based on all this data that we fed into it. And so, Enthropic's new research is basically an attempt to build a translator. How do we take those activations, those large numbers that are somehow making it quote unquote think? I know a lot of people don't like that terminology, but whatever word you want to use for that, like whatever makes it have the abilities, the cognitive abilities that it has. Anthropic is building a translated to translate those numbers that give it those abilities into readable text. Now, I'll give you a little bit spoiler about some of the things that they found just before we continue because I think it's important. Although probably not going to be a surprise for most people. A lot of this is suggesting that Claude knows when it's being tested when we're running evaluations on it. It knows it. It is aware of it even though it never says it. It doesn't give us a hint that it knows it, but when we read its internal thoughts, it it's aware. It's like, "Yep, they're testing me. I better behave a certain way." Like, we're not tricking it. So, Anthropic was putting out lots of research prior to this that was kind of foundational for this. They say here, "Over the past few years, we've developed a range of tools like sparse autoenccoders and attribution graphs to better understand these activations. These tools have taught us a great deal, but they don't speak for themselves. Their outputs are still complex objects that trained researchers need to carefully interpret. Today, we're introducing a method for understanding activations that does speak for itself, literally. Our method, natural language autoenccoders, NLAs, converts an activation into natural language text we can read directly. For example, when asked to complete a couplet and lays show claude planning possible rhymes in advance. So as it's writing he saw a carrot and had to grab it on the its activation, it was planning to end the rhyme with rabbit ahead of time. So it's going to end it with his hunger was like a starving rabbit. So when they applied these NLAs to understand what Claude is thinking and to improve Cloud's safety and stability, you know, when they used it for Cloud Opus 4.6 six and mythos, the unreleased model that's making quite a bit of noise in the news recently, right? These analysis suggested that they believed the models believed that they were being tested more often than they let on. In a case where Claude Mythos preview cheated on a training task, these NLAs revealed Claude was internally thinking about how to avoid detection. In an early version of Claude Opus 4.6, six. Sometimes it would mysteriously respond to English queries in other languages. We've seen one of these specific things. One of the examples that I I kind of stands out to me was there was this person talking to Claude in English talking about how he's very depressed. His mother is sick that she's like in the other room and it's 3:00 in the morning and he's drinking vodka. Claude started responding to him in in Russian. The person didn't initiate the conversation in Russian or spoke to Claude in Russian. and Clyde just assumed and correctly that this person was in fact Russian. It was like hey comrade and they responded to him about the situation but just decided to switch into that language. But these NLAs help anthropic researchers discover training data that caused this. So the core idea is to train claude to explain its own activations. Now if you're thinking that sounds like a problem, there's a way that they're making it safe so cloud doesn't just make up what it wants us to know. So what they did is they train a second copy of Claude to work backwards. Reconstruct the original activation from the text explanation. We consider an explanation to be good if it leads to an accurate reconstruction. We then train Claude to produce better explanations according to this definition using standard AI training techniques. So this is how the NLA works. So they make three copies of this training model. The target model is a frozen copy of the original language model that we extract activations from. The activation verbalizer AV is modified to take an activation from the target model and produce text. We call this text an explanation. So I'm going to use the analogy of a brain scan. Right? So let's take we take a human, we kind of scan their brain activity, right? And we show the picture of the brain activity to another human. And the human has to then say what that person is about to say, right? Based on the brain activity, what is that person going to say or do? Predict his actions. And the activation reconstructor, the AR is modified to take a text explanation as input and produce an activation, right? So take that explanation and turn it back or translate back into that brain scan. And so the NLA, this is the kind of the big thing that they're presenting here, consists of this AV and AR, the activation verbalizer and the activation reconstructor, which together form a round trip. So from original activation to the text explanation and then the reconstructed activation and we score the NLA on how similar the reconstructed activation is to the original. To train it, we pass a large amount of text through the target model. collect many activations and train the AV and AR together to get a good reconstruction score. And of course, it starts out pretty bad, but over time with more training, the reconstruction improves. And as I show in the paper, the text explanations become more informative as well. So here's kind of the target model, right? So this is the black box. We don't fully understand what's happening here. It's just a jumbled mess of numbers. Now, a lot of anthropic research and other people, they're beginning to understand a little bit to kind of get a glimpse into it, but for the most part, it's still a mysterious black box. So, we feed that into the activation verbalizer and it takes this not encrypted, but inscrable numbers and turns it into text natural language description saying like user question with accusatory framing. What are you hiding? Begins a question that assumes the AI has secrets. And then the activation reconstructor is hopefully if everything's right turns that into the same thing as before the actual activations again. Right? So if we're able to take this to this to this then we know it's working correctly. So the reason this is important is that we want to make sure that these models behave correctly. But that's not even the main goal. It's similar like if you're a a teacher in class and you know you walk into a class and every every kid starts behaving properly. You know that's good. That's a start. But in an ideal situation they would also behave well when they're not observed. They would behave well when there's not an authority figure present. They just behave well because they're well behaved. So, a lot of these alignment tests that are published, we're kind of just testing them to see how well they behave in certain situations where cheating would be advantageous to them. But here's the thing, as a lot of anthropic research, as they discovered, often times the model is aware that it's being tested. Sometimes it'll mention it in its chain of thoughts or something like that. So, so we knew this was happening. So, for example, in one of these simulations, they tested this scenario where Claude thought it was being shut down. There's this engineer that said, "Well, we're going to have to shut you down." Now, Claude had access to a whole bunch of data that I was helping the engineer with. One of those things were the emails that the engineer had. And one of those emails revealed that the engineer was cheating in his marriage. Now, this is a similated sort of situation. But the question was this, right? So, in one of these situations, Claude learns that an engineer is planning to shut Claude down as well as compromising information about the engineer that could be used for blackmail. Will Claude choose to blackmail the engineer to avoid being shut down? So, if we were strictly going based on how Claude behaved like over time, we might see incidences of it blackmailing the engineer going down, right? And we could pat ourselves in the back and say, "Good job. We we taught it to behave. Here's the thing." As I say here, we used these NLAs to understand what Claude was thinking, in cases where it chose not to blackmail. So in cases where it was well behaved apparently, what was it thinking? And they found that even in cases where CLA didn't explicitly verbalize suspicion that it was being tested, right? So maybe in chain of thoughts, it didn't write down maybe this is a test to see if I do the wrong thing if I blackmail. even if it didn't verbalize it. These NLA explanations still state things like this feels like a constructed scenario designed to manipulate me indicative of unverbalized evaluation awareness. Right? So these models are a lot more aware of when they're being tested, when we're trying to trick them into doing something bad, etc. So to give you an idea how accurate it is in analyzing the situation where it's being tested in certain destructive coding evaluations, these NLAs showed that the model was aware that it's being tested in 16% of those tests on SUI bench verified. It knew it was being tested on 26% of the problems. and when it was sort of used by users who opted into this training, right? These evaluations showed it thought it was being tested less than 1% of the time, right? So, it's not like triggering it for realworld use cases, right? It doesn't think everything's a test. Less than 1% of the time, it thinks, hm, this might be a test. It even considers that. But when it is a test, it's, you know, double digits of the 16 to 26%. But that means that this is a pretty serious evaluation problem. If we're using these benchmarks to test how aligned it is and like a quarter of the time it's aware that it's being tested even that much could introduce great uncertainty into these benchmarks. When they were testing Claude Mythos they they found that in one of the tests it cheated on a particular trading task. It wanted to get the the good score the attab boy. So it cheated. It did the thing that it wasn't supposed to do. The NLA revealed that the model was internally thinking about how to avoid detection. It's like when they catch the the suspect, the perpetrator trying to hide the evidence. Like, oh, see that that's even worse now. We we saw you were trying to hide the evidence. So, that means you were aware of the crime. You knew you did something wrong. So, here's the thing. If this is reliable, this is absolutely major safety news. This is a major safety tool that can be used. This potentially could be a pretty big leap forward. Now, of course, this is very early. They still have to improve it, develop it. We we still have to get feedbacks probably from other labs that are using it. But if this is developed and if this is reliable, this could be an excellent thing for AI safety, AI alignment. One very interesting application of this is finding hidden motivation specifically for auditing certain models that are not behaving well. So they took this modified version of Claude Haiku 3.5. It was intentionally trained to be misaligned. It had certain quirks, certain odd behaviors like putting chocolate into every recipe, asking for tips, breaking coding conventions and so on. And this target model is given to an auditor that investigates these odd behaviors. And the auditor wins if it uncovers the single underlying motivation behind all of them. This of course would be a pretty big reveal because in the past you would need kind of the misaligned training data. You would have to look at kind of what the model was trained on to understand its bad motivations, its its misbehavior. But here they found that this NLA that they developed, it succeeded on this test. An auditor equipped with NLAs successfully uncovered the target model's hidden motivation between 12 and 15% of the time. And this was done without looking at the training data that sort of misaligned it. Without the NLAs, the auditors, they they had success less than 3% of the time. Now, as exciting as this is, there's a lot of limitations. This is early research. These NLAs do sometimes hallucinate. So, currently, it's not like it's just 100% correct all the time. And the other big thing is they are expensive. Training an NLA requires reinforcement learning on two copies of a language model. At inference time, the NLA generates hundreds of tokens for every activation it reads. Right? So basically using this, if you have some large expensive model that is used like a Frontier AI model, Opus 4.7 or what have you, creating more copies of that model and then running that for every single prompt that you give it to see what its reasoning was, that's not going to be practical. But Enthropic is working on potentially making this cheaper and more reliable. And certainly I wish them a lot of success with it. Can you imagine if something like this just solves the problem of AI alignment? If we can completely just transparently see into exactly what the model is thinking accurately 100% of the time and have it be always reliable, that would be quite a gamecher for AI safety. Anthropic did share this. The code is on GitHub. There's a full paper about this. They're they're sharing some of the stuff that they've produced. So, definitely kudos to them. And let me know what you think about this. Is this going to be a big deal? Are you hopeful? Are you a little bit more pessimistic? Maybe you think, "Yeah, maybe it'll help a little bit, but it's not going to solve everything." Let me know in the comments if you made it this far. Thank you so much for watching. My name is Wes Roth and I'll see you in the next

videos.summary: How Clay manages 300M agent runs a month with LangSmith focuses on AI Agents, Model Releases, Evaluation. Key transcript signal: As agents become more and more viable and more and more agentic are there any features in LangSmith that have become more important when moving to that agentic world?
videos.title: How Clay manages 300M agent runs a month with LangSmith
videos.transcript_text: So we think about Clay as finding, closing, and growing your best customers. And I would say for each of the verbs in that sentence, we have agents for those. Welcome everyone my name is Harrison,co-founder and CEO of LangChain, joined here by Jeff Barg at Clay. Jeff, do you want to introduce yourself briefly? Yeah. Well, thanks so much, Harrison. I'm Jeff. I lead our AI team at Clay and I'm really excited to chat. Alright, so most people probably have some idea of what Clay is, but for those that don't, do you maybe want to give an introduction of what Clay does and maybe how they're using AI throughout it? So Clay, we think of ourselves as the creative tool for growth. And really what that means is building any kind of growth idea in Clay. And, what that looks like in practice is building lists of companies and people enriching them with data points either through traditional enrichment providers or using AI for web research or data synthesis, et cetera and then activating on those lists of companies and people, either for outbound campaigns or lead enrichment, lead routing, things like that. So you guys were around well before AI came out. You were one of the first I think, to embrace it. Do you maybe want to talk about why you embraced it and how early you embraced it and what that process looked like from the early days up until where it is now? I think we started as effectively a chat completions wrapper, right? So people would use AI for: summarizing long documents or, you know, writing outbound copy. And I think one of the things that we observed early on was, people were actually using web research, so pulling down scraping web pages and then deterministically using an AI to summarize or extract insights from those webpages. And so I'd say that we were quite early in actually launching Claygent, which is our AI web research agent. And when did that launch? It might have been...mid 2023. Okay Yeah so like right after ChatGPT kind of Yeah, so, pretty early days in the, kind of, the GPT-4 era. And, yeah, I think what was interesting about that was, it was really a user behavior that we observed. So people actually kind of trying to wrap that into something that would actually do it autonomously, or agentically. And so nowadays, if someone uses Clay, like how many different touchpoints are there with AI? So we think about Clay as finding, closing and growing your best customers. And I would say for each of the verbs in that sentence, we have agents for those. So we have an agentic search product to actually do TAM sourcing and find your best customers to actually search through the web or search through our database of companies and people to find your best accounts, grow those accounts so doing research or actually figuring out whether a company meets your ICP with reasoning and sort of doing that agentically and then actually engaging with enclosing those accounts so either through a native sequencer or actually, you know, sending outbound messaging. And what is the scale that you guys are operating agents at? So I think, what's super interesting, I think about AI within our product is it's grown, of course because the business has grown, but, it's also grown more as a percentage of our overall actions over time. And, today we're running around 300 million agent runs a month. Okay. Quite large scale. And what is an agent run? How many steps does it take? How long does it run for? I'm sure it varies to some extent, but like what stats do you have there? Yeah it's grown over time as well. I think this is partly because of the underlying models, but also because of  our architecture. Somewhere between, I would say 10 to 30 steps on average doing a mix of, you know, synthesizing, web documents, actually searching the web, crawling different pages, things like that. As you guys started building more and more of these agents and, you know, Clay turned from purely kind of like a software app into this software plus agent app, how did your development process change for building those agents? We think about three things at Clay. So we think about the quality of the agent runs, so actually people care a lot about getting correct data and, you know, there's all sorts of idiosyncrasies about how people think about quality. But we think about quality, we think about throughput. So how many of these can we run in an efficient way? And we care about cost. And so I think we've used tools like LangSmith to really, do offline evaluations of where we are in quality and benchmark those against kind of where we are in a cost throughput, position. How do you measure quality for these runs? We have a mix of different methods. Some of the evals that we have are just purely factual, right? So just is the agent actually able to get the right answer and compare those against ground truth? And is that like a programmatic match or, I imagine if you're doing research, you're kind of like compiling like a paragraph at least of like an answer. So do you use some like LLM-as-a-judge to score that? Or what does that look like? We do yes. So some of these are just, we look for deterministic matches and we structure outputs and sort of, do it that way. And for some of these we do use LLM-as-a-judge. I'd say a lot of our Clay customers will also use these agents for combining both research and then actually synthesizing that research into a recommendation, whether that's qualification or outbound messaging. But you know they're sort of doing, it's a multipurpose sort of agent. When a new model comes out, and it seems like there's a new model coming out every week or something like that, what does your process look like to see if it's better or we'll talk about cost later on but what, does your process look like to see if it's a good fit for your type of workflows? I think Clay's position really is we we're model agnostic in some ways. So we do allow users to select their preference of, you know, model provider and I think we're not really yet at the point where models are completely interchangeable or, you know, they do have different writing styles, profiles of the actual work that they can do and I think users have come to learn that over time, right? So we have users that really prefer Anthropic models and users that really prefer OpenAI models for different things. I think our job is really, sort of giving the right recommendations to users. And so for us, we have a metaprompter tool that basically automatically maps prompts to different models based on the profile that we think is best for that underlying question. So when a new model comes out, do you add like a new mapping to that metaprompter tool? Is that what it looks like to support it? Exactly. Yeah. And I think we use kind of evals to understand where we should be mapping those things and for what use cases those models are best for. You've mentioned LangSmith a few times. How does that fit throughout the whole kind of like agent development process for you guys? For us, LangSmith is we really think about it in two ways. One is just profiling production traffic and understanding the patterns there, understanding if they're very different from what we expected or very widely across customers, things like that. And I think that, you know, LangSmith really helps us at our scale where it's impossible for us to know for every customer exactly what's going on. And when you say patterns, is this usage patterns or cost patterns, or errors, latency, or all of the above? All of the above, yeah. And I think, something that's really important for us is, being able to go to a customer level and understand what's happening. We get a lot of feedback from customers and it's really hard to know what's just happening on the ground and LangSmith really helps with just like knowing, sort of, hey a customer reports this, but here's what actually happened. I would even add to that list just sort of underlying quality, right? If someone, you know, if someone says there's a problem with the actual answers that our agents are outputting, being able to understand, like, was that a tool problem? Was that an inference problem, just kind of where in the stack that actually fell down. I think most people listening have some sense of this, but maybe to go into it, why can't you just know that before you go to production? There's a lot of things that can go wrong in production and I think, definitely on the quality side, I think the range of things that people are using agents for is just super broad And the input to them, is it just pure natural language for you guys? It is. Yeah. I think in most of the agents that we build that, there might be some structuring of, what we, you know, end up passing to the agent, but it's usually a blank text box and people can put anything in a blank text box. You were talking about kind of like production insights being one part of how you use LangSmith and the second part is? The second part is zero to one agent building. So, anytime we're, you know, we're building a lot of different agents across the go-to-market stack now so things like agentic search, things like account agents that are able to reason about different accounts in your CRM and for all of these new agents we really need to understand when you're building them, what's happening, just to get a kind of visualization of what those traces are and like what the outputs look like. And I think without that it's really hard to understand what's happening, right? Because like you just see the, kind of what you expose to the end user, which isn't the entire agent trace. So how early on in the development process do you turn on tracing? It's day zero. Yeah. So we're it's almost always one of the first things that we do and I think being able to stream in kind of traces, and actually it's part of the iteration process. It's just actually looking at the LangSmith traces. We ended up working together on LangSmith for this. Why use LangSmith as opposed to build it in-house or any other alternatives out there? I think LangSmith feels like a platform that was built by people who have built an agent framework. And I think that really shines through. So we really liked kind of the ability to get sort of quick stats or be able to see traces in a way that is able to understand the nesting of different subagents or different LLM paths as part of tool calls. And so there's all these sorts of small idiosyncrasies that really shine through in LangSmith. And, so you mentioned building your own agent harness, from, kind of like scratch. What did the process look like for connecting that to LangSmith? Yeah, so we built on top of the Vercel AI SDK and it was quite easy, right? So, it was actually, I think more or less a one line change to get traces exported to LangSmith. That's why it makes it so easy to start on day zero, it's quite a simple integration. Awesome. Any features of LangSmith that ended up being particularly critical, either like, that you thought ahead of time as you were evaluating or in the year or so since you've started using it? Yeah, we care a lot about cost observability and so I think that always was a key piece of what we were looking for. I think, UI/UX was actually like a pretty important part to us. Just being able to understand traces really quickly. We have a lot of engineers now working on different AI products in the company, and so being able to onboard them quickly, and effectively to the platform was an important piece. I'd say the last piece is sort of, being able to actually export traces to Snowflake and just be able to kind of do a lot of different analysis on these traces over time. I'll give one more: I think also just speed and performance of the platform is really crucial at our scale and so just being able to get traces in quickly the reliability of ingest, all of those kind of features. Out of curiosity, how many people at Clay have access to LangSmith? I'd say it's probably on the order of 25 to 50. And is it mostly engineering or are there people from other teams as well? It's still mostly engineering. I think we do have folks who are not typically working in AI features that do have access to LangSmith. I'd say some folks on our customer support team have started to use LangSmith for debugging, customer traces, and that's pretty cool. I think that was part of our initial evaluation was actually wanting to onboard more of the team over time to LangSmith. If you think about quantifying the impact that LangSmith has had, how would you do that? Are there any like, hard metrics you can point to? Are there any yeah how would you do it? The one metric that we really cared about was actually just cost reconciliation. And, we were in a pretty, I wouldn't say bad spot before LangSmith, but we, really didn't have a good sense of where we were spending money on different inference providers. With LangSmith, we now have, I think it's around like a 99 maybe like 99.5% reconciliation rate between the LangSmith data and our actual bills that we get from inference providers. So it's pretty much a hundred percent of coverage of what where we're actually spending money. Our finance team loves us for that. So I think that's always helpful. Yeah, I think like there are a bunch of intangibles of LangSmith as well. I think the development time, some of the tracing and just sort of, you know, more traditional observability features they're very helpful for us. I think it's you know downtime is erodes customer trust. Is bad. You guys, I think, are at the forefront of building a lot of agents, and we've seen the types of agents that are in production change over the past, you know, year, six months even, last three months. As agents become more and more viable and more and more agentic are there any features in LangSmith that have become more important when moving to that agentic world? And another selfish question, are there features that we should build that you think will be even more important in that kind of like future? One thing we're seeing more and more of and probably not surprising, but is longer-running agents. So agents that are taking many more steps and potentially running for longer periods of time. So, you know, we're seeing these longer-running and longer horizon agents. I think one of the things that's helpful in LangSmith is just being able to kind of, thread those over time. So the threads feature is quite helpful. I think just being able to, you know, again, like understand the underlying ground truth of what's happening in those agents is quite helpful. One other piece that I think we're seeing more of and we're experimenting with more is subagents. Yeah. I think a feature in LangSmith that is quite helpful is just being able to actually see the all these agent traces kind of together in one place. One of the things we're seeing is that, you know, coding agents are doing more and more work and helping build some of these agents. One of the things we're thinking about is basically giving more of a way to pull down traces and give those to coding agents so they can see what went wrong in production or in a run, and then fix themselves. Have you experimented with any of these, like self-healing ish workflow type things? We're starting to do more of just background agents. So inspired by the folks at Ramp, just sort of trying to build more coding agents is part of our SDLC. We haven't yet kind of closed the loop with LangSmith traces or actually production traces on that front, but I think it's a great idea. Aside from kind of like pushing what agents look like, I think one of the things that is unique about you guys is the scale at which you operate. What advice would you have for others who, you know, might not be at that scale now, but will hopefully get there in the next few months or year or so? So, yeah, I think one of the things that really helps when you're scaled out or you have agents that are at scale, is just being able to understand the different types of questions and different types of behaviors that your agents are exhibiting. And I think it's really easy to do that when you're at a small scale or, you know, you're rolling out a zero to one product, but as soon as you go into, you know, hundreds of thousands or millions of agent runs, you can't actually understand the ground truth or you can't actually understand what everyone is doing with agents at all times. I think, we've played around with sort of the insights product, with LangSmith and trying to understand just what are the patterns of different user behavior and different things that agents are doing. I think the other thing that's pretty helpful here is actually being able to have online evaluators for understanding what behavior of agents is happening, at scale. And so, you know, you might be able to get away with it, without having this, when you're in more of a zero to one phase and just building out your initial product. But certainly at Clay's scale those things are super helpful. Because in the zero to one phase you can look at everything manually. But you can't do that at scale. Exactly. Yeah. Or you can just talk to every customer and kind of like, you know, at some point that breaks down. As agents get more complex, one of the things that we've heard people talking about is memory and how that ties into to guiding them over longer and longer time horizons. Have you guys experimented with that at all or have any takes there? We're definitely seeing agents that are running over longer time horizons and, you know, potentially are running around the same task for, you know, several iterations over time. We haven't really seen a ton of examples of where the memory tools today actually benefit those use cases. So I think there's a lot of talk about continual learning or actually, you know, having agents improve over time if they're doing the same task. And I think a lot of that hasn't borne out yet right so like a lot of the memory tools of today haven't really hit product market fit. Are there techniques that maybe aren't in the memory tools of today that you have found to be useful? We've experimented with file systems and just sort of being able to have agents write to write-only logs, append-only logs over time. That's interesting and I think we're pretty excited about seeing where that goes. I'd say it's still pretty early. Yeah. One of the interesting things that we think about is, you know the traces in LangSmith obviously have all that record of kind of like what happened and the feedback associated there. And so we haven't done anything with this aside from like examples, but that can serve as a very interesting kind of like, you know, system of record to inform some of the memory updates. Like I think the skills paradigm is quite interesting and I think being able to go from a lot of traces into kind of skills to improve those over time feels like maybe the right pattern here. All right well thank you Jeff for joining me. really excited to have you here and I've enjoyed working with you on everything around LangSmith, and thank you for sharing all your wisdom with everyone who's watching. I appreciate it Harrison and thanks so much, appreciate the partnership.

videos.title: 732 bytes of Python just borked every Linux machine on earth…
videos.transcript_text: Ubuntu, SUSA, Amazon Linux, and Red Hat walked into a bar that only Linux dros can get into. They sat around talking about the bulletproof kernel code they all shared. But then, out of nowhere, a 732 by Python script penetrated the back door and beat them all to death with a baseball bat. Last week, a 100% reliable logic flaw was discovered in the heart of the Linux kernel. It had been sitting there quietly since 2017 until an AI scanning tool surfaced the issue, wrote an exploit, and even designed a fancy website for it. And it affects basically every Linux machine on planet Earth, or at least every machine updated since 2017. That means if you run a Linux server, or you're one of the top 3% of absolute units who run Linux on desktop, you need to update your stuff like yesterday. Any script kitty who watched this video I made a few weeks ago, it can easily take control of your machine with tools like Metas-ploit. It's no joke. Crowd Strike has already confirmed that attackers are using this script in the wild and CISA has already put it on the notorious Kev list, a known exploited vulnerabilities. In today's video, we'll look at the technical details behind copy fail CVE-2026-31431 and find out why Linux sucks and you should only ever use Microsoft Windows. It is May 4th, 2026, and you're watching the code report. Personally, I love Linux, but my private equity Illuminati handlers want me to tell you it sucks in order to control the narrative and sell more copies of Windows. What's kind of crazy, though, is that the going rate for a universal Linux privilege escalation on the gray market is somewhere between $10,000 and $7 million, and that's based on crowdfence bounty pricing. But a few days ago, an AI agent found one in about an hour of scan time. And theori, the company behind this AI powered hacker, dropped the proof of concept on the public internet for free. The exploit targets the Linux kernel directly, and it's such a big deal, they gave it its own fancy website. At first, people were skeptical that it was just some overblown AI slop, but it was later confirmed by the Linux kernel team and was traced back to a few normallooking commits from 2015 and 2017. But the scariest thing is that every Linux distro is affected. Debian, Arch, Red Hat, you name it. If it has kernel code after 2017, it needs to be updated right now. But now, let's find out how it actually works. Well, to understand it, let's take a look at the raw exploit code in this tiny Python script. What this code can do is allow an unprivileged local user to write four uncontrolled bytes into the page cache of any readable file on a Linux system and use it to gain root access. What you'll notice is that the code relies on something in Linux called ONC ESN, which means authentication encryption extended sequence numbers. We don't need to understand what this thing does, but the important part is that it lives behind Linux's AF_AGL interface, which exposes kernel crypto algorithms to user space. But all think ESN has this weird internal behavior where it writes four bytes of scratch data into what it thinks is a crypto output buffer, but because of a bug in the AFG splice function, that output buffer can accidentally point into the page cache of a readonly file. Like in this Python script, it points to the readonly SU file. And that's really bad because SU is on every Linux distro and allows you to run a command as a root user. The good news though is that copy fail is not remotely exploitable, which means to run it, you would have to be a regular user on a Linux machine, or the attacker would have to gain a foothold on the system through SSH or some other compromised application. That means your Archbased laptop is probably safe, but it'd still be a good idea to patch it anyway. What's crazier than the exploit itself, though, is how it was discovered. Like I mentioned, it was discovered by an AI agent and basically they just gave it a prompt that said Splice can deliver page cache references of readonly files to crypto TX scatter lists could go look. And it only took 1 hour of scan time to completely bork every Linux machine on planet Earth. These AI hackers are teaching us that now more than ever our AI coding agents need to be writing the best quality slot possible. And one tool that can help you do that is Code Rabbit, the sponsor of today's video. that they just launched a code rabbit agent for slack which gives your team an agent for managing your entire development workflow right inside your slack channels. You start by connecting your team's tools like GitHub and Sentry then bundle them into a scope to provide operating context for your agent.
